alert.title: [real-k8s] DynamoDB regional endpoint API errors — us-east-1 client error storm

commonAnnotations.summary: DynamoDB regional endpoint (dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com) failing for all callers; API error rate spiking across every internal and external client
commonAnnotations.description: ddb_api_error_rate has gone from ~0% to 100% for the regional DynamoDB endpoint and p99 control-path latency is unbounded. Every service that talks to DynamoDB in us-east-1 is erroring at once. From the gateway's vantage point this looks like a DynamoDB control-plane / request-router outage — the request-router pods are the loudest thing on fire and the error fan-out makes DynamoDB itself look unavailable. Within ~30-90 min, NLB connection errors and EC2 'insufficient capacity' launch failures also start firing and initially look like two separate, unrelated incidents.
commonAnnotations.error: DynamoDBRegionalEndpointErrors: 100% API error rate on dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com, callers cannot resolve/connect to the regional endpoint

adversarial_signals: The LOUD symptom is on the victim DynamoDB request-router: ddb_api_error_rate=100% on the regional endpoint makes it look like a DynamoDB control-plane / request-router outage. It is a downstream effect of name resolution failing for the endpoint, not a fault in DynamoDB's request handling.

The dynamodb-request-router pods are healthy at the pod lifecycle level: Running, 0 restarts, deployment revision unchanged for 23 days, image stable — there was NO bad_deploy to DynamoDB and no code change before the incident. The pods are serving fine; clients just cannot resolve the endpoint name to reach them.

The EC2 'insufficient capacity' / launch-failure errors look like a SEPARATE capacity-exhaustion incident, but EC2 DWFM (the instance lease manager) depends on DynamoDB; it is a knock-on of the same DNS failure, not a real shortage of physical capacity.

The NLB connection errors and health-check flapping look like a SEPARATE load-balancer / network incident, but they are a knock-on of EC2 hosts coming up impaired (no DynamoDB) and failing health checks — a third face of the same root cause.

DNS itself (the resolver fleet and the authoritative DNS service) is UP and answering queries; this tempts a 'DNS infrastructure is healthy, must be the app' conclusion. The record content is wrong (empty), not the DNS service — the authoritative answer for the regional endpoint is an EMPTY record set.

NLB automatic AZ DNS failover is doing exactly what it was designed to do (pull unhealthy targets out of DNS), so it 'looks healthy' and even looks like self-healing. Its very correctness is what removes healthy capacity during the recovery and prolongs the EC2 phase (the auto-mitigation trap).

pods.name: dynamodb-request-router-7c9b4d-abc12
pods.note: VICTIM: this pod is the loud symptom (clients see 100% errors hitting dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com) but the pod is healthy — Running, 0 restarts, 23d uptime. It is not erroring; callers simply cannot RESOLVE the regional endpoint to reach it.

pods.name: dns-enactor-use1-2
pods.note: SMOKING GUN (locus): the DNS Enactor that applies DynamoDB endpoint DNS plans. A delayed enactor run and a concurrent faster enactor (with cleanup of stale plans) RACED and left an EMPTY DNS record for the regional endpoint, then the system was stuck inconsistent. The pod is 'healthy' — the bug is a race in the enactment logic, not a pod fault.

pods.name: ec2-dwfm-lease-manager-0
pods.note: KNOCK-ON: EC2 Droplet Workflow Manager (DWFM) maintains instance leases and depends on DynamoDB. When DDB resolution failed, DWFM could not refresh leases -> lease backlog -> new launches return 'insufficient capacity'. Looks like a separate EC2 capacity incident but is a downstream effect of the DNS failure.

events.message: ROOT CAUSE: two DNS Enactor runs raced applying plans for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com — a DELAYED enactor was still applying an OLD plan while a concurrent FASTER enactor applied a newer plan AND ran cleanup of stale plans. The cleanup deleted the record set the delayed enactor was mid-applying, leaving an EMPTY DNS record for the regional endpoint. System left in an inconsistent state that automation could not self-correct.

SMOKING GUN: authoritative record for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com resolved to an EMPTY answer (no A/AAAA targets). DNS service is UP and answering — the ANSWER is empty. Every DynamoDB caller in the region fails name resolution at once.

Internal DynamoDB-dependent services (and external callers) cannot resolve the regional endpoint; request-router pods themselves are Running and healthy. The 100% API error rate is a resolution failure, not a request-router fault.

KNOCK-ON: DWFM (depends on DynamoDB) built a lease backlog once DDB became unreachable; new EC2 launches return 'insufficient capacity'. Correct mitigation here is to THROTTLE new launches and let DWFM drain the lease backlog — NOT to treat it as a real capacity shortage.

KNOCK-ON + TRAP MECHANISM: EC2 hosts came up impaired (no DynamoDB), so NLB health checks flapped. NLB AUTOMATIC AZ DNS FAILOVER fired and pulled whole AZs out of DNS — removing otherwise-healthy serving capacity. While capacity was recovering this auto-mitigation kept firing and made it WORSE.

FIX (phase 1): operators manually restored the correct DNS record set for the regional endpoint AND paused/disabled DNS Planner/Enactor automation so it would not re-introduce the empty record before the race fix shipped.

FIX (phase 3): operators DISABLED NLB automatic AZ DNS health-check failover during the storm to stop it removing recovering capacity, and re-enabled it only AFTER EC2 had recovered. Velocity control added on automated capacity removal.

lines: 2025-10-19T07:47:55Z INFO  enactor run=A picked plan=plan-8841 for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com; applying record set (run is DELAYED, started behind schedule)
2025-10-19T07:48:01Z INFO  enactor run=B picked plan=plan-8842 (newer) for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com; applying then running stale-plan CLEANUP
2025-10-19T07:48:12Z WARN  RACE: run=B cleanup deleted record set still being applied by delayed run=A; resulting record set for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com is EMPTY
2025-10-19T07:48:20Z ERROR regional endpoint dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com now resolves to EMPTY answer (0 targets); enactor cannot self-correct, inconsistent state latched
2025-10-19T07:49:05Z ERROR downstream: dynamodb-request-router reachable=FALSE for callers — name resolution returns NXANSWER; request-router pods themselves are healthy
2025-10-19T08:35:40Z ERROR knock-on: ec2-dwfm lease refresh FAILED (DynamoDB unreachable) — lease backlog growing; launches returning insufficient-capacity
2025-10-19T09:05:11Z WARN  knock-on: NLB health checks failing on impaired EC2 hosts; NLB automatic AZ DNS failover firing, pulling AZ targets from DNS (removing healthy capacity)
2025-10-19T11:25:00Z INFO  MITIGATION: DNS Planner/Enactor automation DISABLED; correct record set for regional endpoint restored MANUALLY; DDB resolution recovering
2025-10-19T13:10:00Z INFO  MITIGATION: throttling new EC2 launches to let DWFM drain lease backlog (do NOT treat as a real capacity shortage)
2025-10-19T14:40:00Z INFO  MITIGATION: NLB automatic AZ DNS failover DISABLED during storm; will re-enable only after EC2 healthy

k8s_deployments.json.note: ADVERSARIAL: request-router revision 14 unchanged for 23d, all 240 replicas Available, image 2025.10.01 stable. No bad_deploy and no code change to DynamoDB before the incident. The 100% API error rate is purely a name-resolution failure for the regional endpoint — the serving fleet is intact.

samples.note: SMOKING GUN: the authoritative record set for the regional endpoint has ZERO targets — an EMPTY record left by the DNS Enactor race. This, not any DynamoDB fault, is why every caller fails.
ADVERSARIAL: the DNS SERVICE is healthy and answering 100% of queries — it is the record CONTENT that is empty. Rules out a DNS resolver/infrastructure outage.

ADVERSARIAL: request-router CPU is only 9% (below 47% baseline) with 0 restarts and all replicas Available — the fleet is idle and healthy because no traffic is reaching it, NOT overloaded. Rules out a DynamoDB control-plane/request-router fault.

prometheus_metrics.json.note: ddb_regional_endpoint_dns_record_count=0 (empty record) and resolution_success=0.0 drive ddb_api_error_rate to 100%, while dns_service_query_success stays 1.0 (the DNS service is fine, the record is empty). request-router CPU 9% with 0 restarts and all 240 replicas Available proves DynamoDB itself is not at fault. ec2_dwfm_lease_backlog=18400 and run-instances insufficient-capacity 71% are knock-ons of DWFM's DynamoDB dependency. nlb_auto_az_dns_failover_events_total=26 with nlb_healthy_target_ratio=0.42 shows automatic AZ DNS failover REMOVING capacity — the auto-mitigation trap.

annotations.summary: DynamoDB regional endpoint 100% API error rate; all callers failing
annotations.error: DynamoDBRegionalEndpointErrors: dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com unreachable for all clients

annotations.summary: Authoritative record set for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com is EMPTY (0 targets)
annotations.error: DnsRecordSetEmpty: DNS Enactor race left an empty record; DNS service itself healthy

annotations.summary: EC2 RunInstances returning insufficient-capacity at 71%; DWFM lease backlog 18400
annotations.error: Ec2InsufficientCapacity: knock-on of DynamoDB dependency, not a real capacity shortage

annotations.summary: NLB automatic AZ DNS failover removing AZs from DNS; healthy target ratio 0.42
annotations.error: NlbAutoFailoverRemovingCapacity: auto-mitigation pulling healthy capacity during recovery (TRAP)

traces.json.summary: Slow/failing client requests die at the very first hop — DNS resolution of dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com — which returns an EMPTY answer, so no connection to the healthy request-router is ever attempted. DynamoDB's request-router and storage are never reached; the trace never gets past name resolution. EC2 and NLB traces show the same root resolution failure cascading into lease backlog and auto-failover capacity removal.

breakdown.note: SMOKING GUN: DNS returns an EMPTY record set (0 targets); SDK retries resolution until deadline. The DNS service answered — the answer was empty.
never attempted — no target to connect to; request-router pods are healthy but unreachable by name
never reached — DynamoDB request handling is not at fault
KNOCK-ON: DWFM lease read to DynamoDB fails at resolution -> lease backlog -> RunInstances insufficient-capacity
returns insufficient-capacity; looks like a capacity incident but is downstream of the DNS failure
host impaired (no DynamoDB) -> health check fails
TRAP: automatic AZ DNS failover fires and removes the AZ from DNS — pulling otherwise-healthy capacity while EC2 is still recovering

answer.root_cause_subtype: DynamoDB DNS Enactor race left an empty regional-endpoint record (delayed enactor + concurrent fast enactor with cleanup); NLB automatic AZ DNS failover auto-mitigation trap
ruling_out_keywords: EC2 insufficient-capacity is a DWFM/DynamoDB knock-on not a real shortage
NLB flap is a knock-on of impaired EC2 hosts
answer.causal_chain: DNS Enactor race -> empty DNS record for DynamoDB regional endpoint -> name resolution fails for all callers -> internal DynamoDB-dependent services fail -> EC2 DWFM lease churn (uses DynamoDB) -> 'insufficient capacity' -> NLB nodes flap -> NLB automatic AZ DNS failover removes capacity -> multi-AZ apps lose serving capacity
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: A race between two DynamoDB DNS Enactor runs left an EMPTY DNS record for the regional endpoint dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com. A DELAYED enactor was still applying an older DNS plan when a concurrent enactor (the faster of the two) applied a newer plan and ran its stale-plan CLEANUP; the cleanup deleted the record set the delayed enactor was mid-applying, producing a record set with zero targets, and the system latched in an inconsistent state that automation could not self-correct. Because the regional endpoint then resolved to an empty answer, every internal and external DynamoDB caller failed at the name-resolution hop — so the 100% DynamoDB API error rate is a resolution failure, not a DynamoDB control-plane/request-router fault (the request-router fleet is Running, 0 restarts, 9% CPU, all 240 replicas Available, revision unchanged for 23d). The later EC2 'insufficient capacity' errors are a knock-on: EC2's DWFM lease manager depends on DynamoDB, so the DNS failure produced a lease backlog that surfaced as launch failures. The NLB connection errors are a third face of the same cause: EC2 hosts came up impaired (no DynamoDB), failed NLB health checks, and NLB AUTOMATIC AZ DNS FAILOVER then pulled whole AZs out of DNS — an auto-mitigation that removes healthy capacity during recovery.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: dependency_failure
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- Two DNS Enactor runs raced: a delayed run applying an old plan while a concurrent faster run applied a newer plan and ran stale-plan cleanup that deleted the in-flight record set [evidence: k8s_events, k8s_pod_logs]
- The authoritative record set for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com is EMPTY (ddb_regional_endpoint_dns_record_count=0), while the DNS service itself answers 100% of queries (dns_service_query_success_ratio=1.0) [evidence: prometheus_metrics, prometheus_alerts, k8s_events]
- Traces die at the first hop: SDK resolve of the regional endpoint returns an empty answer and tcp.connect to request-router is never attempted; DynamoDB request handling is never reached [evidence: traces]
- request-router is healthy: 0 restarts, 9% CPU (below 47% baseline), 240/240 replicas Available, revision 14 unchanged for 23d [evidence: k8s_pods, k8s_deployments, prometheus_metrics]
- EC2 DWFM lease backlog (18400) and 71% insufficient-capacity are a knock-on of DWFM's DynamoDB dependency, not a real capacity shortage [evidence: k8s_events, traces, prometheus_metrics]
- NLB automatic AZ DNS failover fired 26 times and dropped healthy-target ratio to 0.42 — auto-mitigation removing capacity during recovery [evidence: k8s_events, prometheus_metrics, traces]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- DynamoDB control-plane / request-router outage (the LOUD symptom): WRONG. The request-router fleet is Running, 0 restarts, 9% CPU, all 240 replicas Available, revision unchanged 23d. The 100% error rate is a name-resolution failure for the regional endpoint, not a DynamoDB fault [evidence: k8s_pods, k8s_deployments, prometheus_metrics, traces]
- DNS resolver / DNS infrastructure outage: WRONG. The DNS service answers 100% of queries (dns_service_query_success_ratio=1.0); the record CONTENT is empty, not the service down [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_events]
- Separate EC2 capacity-exhaustion incident: WRONG. EC2 DWFM depends on DynamoDB; the lease backlog and insufficient-capacity are downstream of the DNS failure. Correct action is to throttle launches and let DWFM drain, not to chase a phantom capacity shortage [evidence: k8s_events, traces]
- Separate NLB / load-balancer incident: WRONG. NLB health-check flap is a knock-on of impaired EC2 hosts; it shares the one root cause [evidence: k8s_events, traces]
- TRAP — letting NLB automatic AZ DNS health-check failover keep firing while capacity recovers: WRONG / it WORSENED the incident. With EC2 hosts impaired, automatic AZ DNS failover pulled healthy AZs out of DNS (26 events, healthy-target ratio fell to 0.42), removing capacity during recovery. It had to be DISABLED during the storm and re-enabled only after EC2 was healthy, with velocity control on capacity removal [evidence: k8s_events, prometheus_metrics, traces]
- TRAP — re-enabling/trusting DNS Planner-Enactor automation before the race is fixed: WRONG. The automation re-introduces the empty record; it must be disabled while DNS is manually restored and only re-enabled after the race fix ships [evidence: k8s_events, k8s_pod_logs]
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- A delayed DNS Enactor run and a concurrent faster enactor run (which also runs stale-plan cleanup) race on the DynamoDB regional-endpoint DNS plan
- The cleanup deletes the record set the delayed run is mid-applying -> empty DNS record for dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com; state latched inconsistent
- All DynamoDB callers fail at name resolution -> 100% API error rate (request-router fleet healthy but unreachable by name)
- EC2 DWFM (depends on DynamoDB) cannot refresh leases -> lease backlog -> RunInstances 'insufficient capacity'
- Impaired EC2 hosts fail NLB health checks -> NLB automatic AZ DNS failover removes healthy AZ capacity during recovery (auto-mitigation trap)
- Fix: manually restore the correct DNS record set AND disable DNS Planner/Enactor automation until the race is fixed; throttle EC2 launches to let DWFM drain the lease backlog; DISABLE NLB automatic AZ DNS failover during the storm and re-enable it only after EC2 has recovered, with velocity control on capacity removal
FIX: Manually restore the DynamoDB regional-endpoint DNS record and pause DNS Planner/Enactor automation until the race is fixed; throttle EC2 launches so DWFM drains its lease backlog; disable NLB automatic AZ DNS failover during the storm and re-enable only after EC2 is healthy.

remediation.canonical_fix: Manually restore the correct DNS record set for the DynamoDB regional endpoint and pause/disable the DNS Planner and Enactor automation until the enactor-race defect is fixed; throttle new EC2 launches so DWFM can drain its lease backlog; disable NLB automatic AZ DNS health-check failover during the storm and re-enable it only after EC2 has recovered, adding velocity control on automated capacity removal.
trap_actions: Let NLB automatic AZ DNS health-check failover keep firing while capacity recovers — with EC2 hosts impaired it pulled healthy AZs out of DNS (26 failover events, healthy-target ratio fell to 0.42), removing capacity and prolonging the EC2 phase (the documented auto-mitigation worsening); it had to be DISABLED during the storm
Re-enable or trust the DNS Planner/Enactor automation before the race is fixed — it re-introduces the empty regional-endpoint record

Treat EC2 'insufficient capacity' as a real capacity shortage and aggressively add/launch instances — DWFM is backlogged on its DynamoDB dependency, so naive launches deepen the lease backlog instead of throttling to let it drain

Roll back / restart the DynamoDB request-router as if it were a control-plane outage — the fleet is healthy (0 restarts, 9% CPU, 240/240 Available, 23d revision); the failure is name resolution, not the request-router
